sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Diffusion language models promise parallel generation, yet still lag behind autoregressive (AR) models in quality. We stem this gap to a failure of introspective consistency: AR models agree with their own generations, while DLMs often do not. We define the introspective acceptance rate, which measures whether a model accepts its previously generated tokens. This reveals why AR training has a structural advantage: causal masking and logit shifting implicitly enforce introspective consistency. Motivated by this observation, we introduce Introspective Diffusion Language Model (I-DLM), a paradigm that retains diffusion-style parallel decoding while inheriting the introspective consistency of AR training. I-DLM uses a novel introspective strided decoding (ISD) algorithm, which enables the model to verify previously generated tokens while advancing new ones in the same forward pass. From a systems standpoint, we build I-DLM inference engine on AR-inherited optimizations and further customize it with a stationary-batch scheduler. To the best of our knowledge, I-DLM is the first DLM to match the quality of its same-scale AR counterpart while outperforming prior DLMs in both model quality and practical serving efficiency across 15 benchmarks. It reaches 69.6 on AIME-24 and 45.7 on LiveCodeBench-v6, exceeding LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B) by more than 26 and 15 points, respectively. Beyond quality, I-DLM is designed for the growing demand of large-concurrency serving, delivering about 3x higher throughput than prior state-of-the-art DLMs.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Diffusion language models promise parallel generation, yet still lag behind autoregressive (AR) models in quality. It reaches 69.6 on AIME-24 and 45.7 on LiveCodeBench-v6, exceeding LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B) by more than 26 and 15 points, respectively. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Diffusion language models (DLMs) (Austin et al., 2021; Sahoo et al., 2024; Nie et al., 2025a; Cheng et al., 2025) offer an appealing alternative to autoregressive (AR) language models: by iteratively refining block of tokens, they break the sequential bottleneck of next-token decoding and enable parallel generation. Yet this promise remains largely unrealized, as shown in Figure 1. First, substantial quality gap between DLMs and AR models remains key barrier to adoption (2). Second, from an efficiency standpoint, limited system support for diffusion inference prevents DLMs from translating their theoretical parallelism into practical speedups. The historical trajectory of DLM development is highly revealing. Across broad line of work, from early continuous formulations (Li et al., 2022) and uniform-state DLMs (Austin et al., 2023) to discrete diffusion models (Lou et al., 2024); and from fully bidirectional attention (Nie et al., 2025b), to blockwise decoding (Cheng et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025) and causal-mask decoding (Liu et al., 2025a), each generation moves closer to discrete AR language models. Increasingly AR-like training signals have also been introduced to 1 Preprint. Figure 1: (a) Introspective consistency: standard DLMs generate tokens whose distributions diverge from the models own next-step predictions p; I-DLM trains generation and introspection to agree (p q). (b) Quality vs. throughput on MATH-500: I-DLM-8B matches Qwen3-8B (thinking) AR performance while achieving 3.1 higher throughput and +11.8 points over LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B), and 4.0 higher throughput over SDAR (8B). improve optimization and quality (Liu et al., 2025b; Gat et al., 2025; Ye et al., 2025; Tian et al., 2025). In other words, much of the field has implicitly converged on the same intuition: the path to stronger DLMs should progressively move closer to AR models. In this work, we argue for different trajectory. Rather than beginning from diffusion and asking how to make it more like AR, we begin from AR and ask: what is the essential principle that makes AR models so strong, and can it be preserved in parallel generation paradigm? We revisit this question from two perspectives: algorithms and systems. From the algorithmic perspective, this change in viewpoint leads us to re-examine deceptively simple design at the core of AR modeling: causal masking together with logit shifting. Beyond enabling next-token prediction, this training structure implicitly teaches the model to revisit and validate its previously generated tokens under the same predictive rule. In effect, AR models are trained to agree with what they generate, exposing fundamental limitation of existing DLMs: although they can generate tokens in parallel, they are generally not trained to agree with their own generations (e.g., due to multi-step bidirectional denoising). We formalize this gap through the notion of introspective acceptance rate, which measures whether model internally accepts its previously generated tokens. We find that this property serves as useful proxy for the degree to which DLM remains consistent with its own generations. From the systems perspective, existing DLMs are often optimized for aggressive low-latency decoding, but this comes at the cost of substantially higher computational overhead. While such overhead can be partially hidden in memory-bound regimes, production deployments requiring large-batch inference quickly hit the compute-bound regime. Unfortunately, LLM serving stacks are poorly matched to the multiple-query, multiple-denoising patterns. Contributions. These algorithmic-system mismatches motivate us to co-design the Introspective Diffusion Language Model (I-DLM), new paradigm that preserves the introspective consistency of AR while retaining parallelism. I-DLM is built on introspective-consistency training, an efficient recipe for converting pretrained AR models into DLMs, and novel introspective strided decoding (ISD) algorithm that generates tokens per forward pass while verifying prior tokens against causal anchor distribution. We show that explicitly enforcing introspective consistency during training is key to substantially closing the quality gap between DLMs and strong same-scale AR models. We make the following contributions: key insight: introspective consistency is the missing principle in prior DLMs. We show that diffusion language models do not inherit the introspective consistency of AR models: they are not trained to agree with their own generations. This missing property fundamentally limits their ability to realize the full capability of the underlying model. 2 Preprint. (a) Generationintrospection gap. Introspective acceptance rate (avg min(1, p/q)) between generation (q) and introspection (p) distributions. AR and Ours achieve near-perfect consistency. (b) Compute economics at stride N=4. denotes the per-token acceptance probability. ISD achieves high TPF at low overhead. (c) Batching efficiency. TPF vs. batch throughput (tokens/s) at batch size 8. SDARs throughput barely scales with TPF; I-DLM translates higher TPF into proportionally higher throughput. Figure 2: Bottleneck analysis. Key gaps between DLMs and AR models: (a) existing DLMs exhibit generationintrospection gapthey can generate tokens but cannot reliably introspect on their own output, as measured by the introspection rate; (b) DLM parallel decoding consumes far more compute per token, collapsing throughput under concurrency; (c) higher TPF does not translate to proportionally higher throughput for existing DLMs. new training paradigm for high-quality parallel generation. We introduce introspective-consistency training, simple yet effective recipe for converting pretrained AR models into introspective DLMs (e.g., just using 5B tokens). By explicitly enforcing models introspective consistency, our method enables parallel decoding without sacrificing AR-level quality. Unlike prior approaches, it requires neither distillation schedules nor masking curricula, yielding stable and efficient path to high-quality DLMs. novel single-pass decoding algorithm that unifies generation and verification. We propose Introspective Strided Decoding (ISD), which simultaneously generates new tokens and revises prior ones within the same forward pass. At [MASK] positions, the model proposes new tokens; at introspection positions, it revisits previous tokens against its causal anchor distribution. This yields outputs that provably match the base AR distribution, without confidence heuristics or separate verification passes. An AR-compatible serving stack for deployable DLMs and self-speculative decoding We design an inference stack that is directly compatible to existing AR serving systems (e.g., SGLang). Besides, we develop gated residual adaptation mechanism where LoRA adapters are applied only at mask positions, while verification relies on the base model weights. It supports continuum between near-lossless and strictly lossless modes. Comprehensive evidence that closes the quality gap while delivering real efficiency gains. Across 15 benchmarks, we show that I-DLM is the first DLM to match strong same-scale AR quality while substantially outperforming prior DLMs in both capability and serving efficiency. Our results establish new qualityefficiency frontier. We will open-source models and systems to facilitate community research and deployment.

sections.title: 2 Background and Motivation
sections.content: We trace these gaps to three inherent limitations of current DLMs (Figure 2). (1) Low introspective consistency: DLMs cannot reliably agree with their generations, making coherent reasoning difficult; (2) Compute inefficiency: both training and inference require substantially more FLOPs in tokens per forward (TPF) than AR models; and (3) Inference engine incompatibility: multi-token, multi-step denoising in DLM inference is poorly aligned with modern AR serving stacks, leading to inefficient execution. DLMs lack introspective consistency. We formalize introspective acceptance rate α as follows: for each generated token xk sampled from distribution qk, we perform separate forward pass with all tokens revealed to obtain the corresponding causal distribution pk and compute α = 1 min(1, pk(xk)/qk(xk)). For AR models, = by construction, yielding α = 1 3 Preprint. and thus perfect generation-introspection consistency. We evaluate α on IFEval using each models best configuration from its official release (Figure 2a). SDAR (8B) achieves only 0.699 and LLaDA 2.0-flash (8B) only 0.568, indicating substantial divergence between what these models generate and what they would endorse upon re-examination. LLaDA 2.1 (Bie et al., 2026) is philosophically close to us. Although it is motivated as improving the revision capability of DLMs, we view revision and introspective acceptance as deeply connected: improving models ability to revise its own outputs naturally improves its ability to endorse them upon re-examination. LLaDA 2.1 restructures the training and data pipeline for token-to-token supervision format, whereas our introspective-consistency training is much simpler, jointly training generation and introspection under unified objective without redesigning the data pipeline (3.1). Despite this simplicity, I-DLM achieves substantially higher introspective acceptance rates with far greater token efficiency: it matches its AR base model using only 4.5B training tokens, while SDAR needs 54B tokens (12 more) and still yields much worse downstream quality (10.0 vs. 69.6 on AIME-24). Parallel decoding does not translate to compute efficiency. Existing baselines incur more flops per decoded token, which effectively dilutes the achieved speedup by pushing the kernel to compute bound, as shown in Figure 2b. We define compute overhead as the ratio of total FLOPs to decode given number of tokens compared to AR decoding. We provide detailed tokens per forward (TPF) pass and compute overhead analysis given different per-token acceptance rates in Appendix B. Figure 2b shows the tradeoff at stride N=4 for I-DLM, SDAR (block diffusion model), and Tidar (DLM with branched inference methods): at TPF of 2.5, I-DLM incurs only 2.5 compute overhead, while TiDAR requires 7.8. In contrast, SDARs TPF is capped at 2.0. This is because block diffusion (SDAR, LLaDA) requires denoising steps plus mandatory KV-commit forward that produces no new tokens, capping TPF at N/2 even under ideal acceptance (p=1). Appendix details why this overhead is hard to eliminate. To quantify this tradeoff, we define compute efficiency as TPF/OH; efficiency > 1 means the TPF gain outweighs the overhead cost. Figure 3 plots efficiency against acceptance rate for N=4. ISD is the only method that crosses the break-even line, reaching efficiency > 1 at 0.83 (variable-query) and 0.86 (fixed-query). At our empirically observed rates (p 0.85), ISD achieves 1.082.29 efficiency. In contrast, TiDARs efficiency is capped at N/(N+1) = 0.80 even at = 1, and SDAR remains around 0.640.72 at practical acceptance rates. Figure 3: Compute efficiency (TPF/OH) vs. acceptance rate at N=4. ISD is the only method above the break-even line. DLM inference is incompatible with AR serving engines. The modern LLM serving stack (e.g., continuous batching, fused attention kernels, paged KV cache) is highly optimized for AR decoding. DLMs break these assumptions. In AR serving, continuous batching works because all requests advance uniformly. In block diffusion, tokens within block converge at different rates: some positions pass the confidence threshold early, yet all requests must synchronize at the slowest block, wasting the TPF gain. Figure 2c reflects this: SDARs TPS growth rate (slope=84) is nearly flat with respect to TPF. In contrast, ISD uses strict causal attention (compatible with AR kernels) and adaptive stride, where each step produces at least one quality-guaranteed token via introspection, achieving TPS growth rate of 549 (3.3). We show other AR serving mismatch (e.g., attention kernel mismatch) in Appendix D. We bring up more discussion on detailed related works in Appendix A.

sections.title: Introspective Diffusion Language Model
sections.content: We present I-DLM to address the aforementioned bottlenecks. Causal attention with logit shift closes the generationintrospection gap and improves training efficiency (3.1); introspective strided decoding eliminates the compute overhead of iterative denoising (3.2); and the preserved causal structure enables direct integration into AR serving stacks (3.3). 4 Preprint. 3.1 Introspective-Consistency Training We convert pretrained AR model into an introspective diffusion model by combining causal attention, logit shift, and an all-masked objective. The training mask is shown in Appendix E. We note that our proposed introspective-consistency training method itself is simple, but it is built on deep and fundamental insight inspired by AR: AR training unifies generation and introspection in one forward pass; DLM training should inherit the same spirit. Causal training with logit shift. We apply strict causal masking uniformly across both the masked and clean portions: for any query position and key position i, attention is permitted only when j. Unlike SDAR which uses block-causal attention (bidirectional within blocks), this holds for masked tokens in xt as welleach [MASK] attends only to preceding positions. In the clean region x0, standard causal attention is used to ensure generation and introspection operate under the same attention pattern (Section 2) and enable KV cache reuse at inference. We pair this with logit shift: the hidden state at position is trained to predict token i+1 rather than token i. Standard masked diffusion trains hidden[Mi] token[i], which breaks the AR models inherent logits[i] token[i+1] mapping and crucially prevents clean positions from providing meaningful verify signal. Our shifted formulation preserves this mapping: clean positions produce the causal anchor (the verify distribution), while masked positions produce tokens (the decode distribution). All-masked training with auto-balanced loss. Given clean sequence x0 = (x1, . . . , xL), we replace all tokens with special [MASK] token to obtain the fully masked input xt = (M, . . . , M). The training input is the concatenation [xt x0], where xt is the all-masked sequence and x0 provides the clean reference. Unlike standard masked diffusion training, which masks random fraction of tokens, wasting (1r) of the compute on unsupervised positions, our all-masked regime ensures that every position contributes useful training signal, eliminating the supervision dilution identified in Section 2. We apply cross-entropy loss with shifted labels separately to the masked and clean regions: Lmask = 1 St ℓSt log pθ(xℓ+ 0 [xt, x0]ℓ), Lclean = 1 S0 ℓS0 log pθ(xℓ+1 [xt, x0]ℓ), 0 (1) where St and S0 are the non-padding positions in the masked and clean regions, respectively, and xℓ+1 is the shifted target (next token). This is the same cross-entropy objective used in AR pretrainingno separate distillation loss or teacher model is required. On clean positions, Lclean trains the introspection pathway: the model learns to produce the causal anchor distribution pθ(xi+1 xi), recovering the exact AR training objective. On masked positions, Lmask trains the decode pathway: the model learns to produce tokens from [MASK] hidden states, enabling strided generation at stride > 1. Although both pathways share the same cross-entropy objective, their loss magnitudes can differ substantially: masked positions face harder prediction task and tend to produce larger losses, especially early in training. fixed weighting risks the decode pathway dominating the gradient, undermining the introspection pathway that is critical for verification quality. We address this with an auto-balanced loss: = Lmask + ˆs Lclean, ˆs =

This dynamically rescales the clean-position loss to match the magnitude of the masked-position loss, ensuring both pathways receive equal effective gradient magnitude without manual tuning. Because both pathways share the same objective and receive balanced supervision, the model naturally aligns with p, maximizing the introspective acceptance rate (Figure 2a). note on why previous works fail to achieve introspective consistency: While the individual ingredients of our approach, such as causal-mask training (Hu et al., 2025), logit shifting (Ye et al., 2025), and full-mask training (Liu et al., 2025b), have each been explored 5 Preprint. Figure 4: Comparison of decoding paradigms. Our I-DLM uses strict causal attention with adaptive stride (1 < stride < N) and is drop-in replacement within AR serving infrastructure. ISD produces quality-guaranteed token xi+1 together with draft tokens ˆxi+2:i+N via introspective strided decoding, xi+1, ˆxi+2:i+N = πstrided(c1:i, m1:N1); Residual ISD (R-ISD) additionally gates LoRA residual for bit-for-bit lossless output: xi+1:i+N = πar(c1:i, m1:N1) + πL(m1:N1). in isolation, their combined role in enabling true introspective consistency has remained largely overlooked. We ablate these components in Figure 6a: removing causal attention and logit shift (reverting to block diffusion) causes sharp degradation on reasoning tasks (e.g., HumanEval: 92.7 60.3), confirming that introspective consistency is critical for longhorizon generation. Specifically, causal masking ensures generation-time context consistency across denoising steps, logit shifting further bridges the verification and generation with unified hidden states and ensures training robustness by respecting the autoregressive (AR) models inherent behavior, and the all-masked objective ensures training efficiency through dense supervision; no single component achieves consistency, robustness, and efficiency simultaneously. Prior work such as NBDiff (Tian et al., 2025) adopts causal masking for prefilling and block-causal masking for decoding, and achieves substantial gains. This confirms that even partial generation-time consistency at the prefilling stage alone delivers notable improvements, but switching between masking schemes introduces nontrivial computational overhead at inference time. Similarly, LLaDA-2.1 shares our broader goal of revision during generation, but it depends on heavy data engineering to construct multiturn revision sequences. In contrast, our method provides unified training pathway that ensures consistency, robustness, and efficiency simultaneously by directly distilling the AR models inherent behavior through an efficient single-stage training regime. 3.2 Introspective Strided Decoding (ISD) Introspective Strided Decoding (ISD) is the inference counterpart to I-DLM training. Because the model uses strict causal attention, ISD begins with standard AR prefill over the prompt, then dynamically selects the effective stride at each decode step via the p/q acceptance criterion. Unlike block diffusion which commits to fixed block size, or speculative decoding which requires separate draft model, the stride in ISD adapts intrinsically based on the models own self-consistency: easy tokens are accepted in parallel while difficult tokens fall back toward AR-quality generation. Single-pass stride and introspection. ISD generates tokens in steps (Algorithm 1, Figure 4 bottom): 1 Step 1 (bootstrap). We append [MASK] tokens to the prefix and run single forward pass. Due to the logit shift, the last clean position produces quality-guaranteed token x1identical to an AR prediction, requiring no introspection. This token anchors the subsequent introspection chain: at the next step, x1s hidden state produces the causal anchor p2 for evaluating ˆx2, and so on. The remaining N1 [MASK] positions produce strided tokens ˆx2, . . . , ˆxN; and 2 Step > 1 (stride + introspection). We fill in the accepted (or Preprint. Algorithm 1 Introspective Strided Decoding (one iteration) {strided proposal} (N1 masks) {quality-guaranteed (AR prediction)} Sample xL+k qk softmax(logitsstride[L+k1]) Require: Prefix tokens x1:L, stride N, model with logit shift Ensure: Accepted new tokens appended to prefix 1: // Stride: N1 masks produce tokens (logit shift gives +1 free) 2: Construct input [x1:L, MASK1, . . . , MASKN1] 3: Run logitsstride[1:L+N1] 4: Sample xL+1 softmax(logitsstride[L]) 5: for = 2 to do 6: 7: end for 8: // Introspect: verify proposals against causal anchors 9: // (Only introspection shown; in practice fused with next proposal into one 2N1 token pass) 10: Construct input [x1:L, xL+1, . . . , xL+N] 11: Run logitsanchor[1:L+N] 12: pk softmax(logitsanchor[L+k1]) for = 1, . . . , 13: // Introspection with adaptive stride 14: naccepted 1 15: for = 2 to do 16: 17: 18: 19: 20: 21: end if 22: 23: end for 24: // Bonus token if all proposals accepted 25: if naccepted = then 26: 27: 28: end if 29: 30: Append xL+1:L+naccepted to prefix; commit KV cache Draw Uniform(0, 1) if < min(cid:0)1, pk(xL+k) / qk(xL+k)(cid:1) then Accept xL+k; naccepted naccepted + 1 Resample xL+k normalize(cid:0) max(0, pk qk)(cid:1) naccepted naccepted + 1; break Sample xL+N+1 softmax(logitsanchor[L+N]) naccepted naccepted + 1 {xL+1 always accepted (quality-guaranteed)} {causal anchor distributions} else resampled) tokens from the previous step, append fresh [MASK] tokens, and run single forward pass that simultaneously: Introspects on the previous tokens, the filled-in tokens are now clean, so the logits at each position produce causal anchor distributions pk (the true AR next-token distributions); Generates new tokens from the appended [MASK] positions. Crucially, introspection piggybacks on the next stride step at zero additional cost: every step after the first produces both accepted tokens and fresh tokens in one forward pass. This contrasts with SDAR (T+1 forwards per block), WeDLM (confidence-based streaming without distributional guarantees), and TiDAR (unified draft and introspection but N(N+1) token queries per step; our logit shift reduces this to N2). Adaptive stride via p/q acceptance. We introspect on each token by comparing it against its causal anchor using the p/q acceptance criterion (Leviathan et al., 2023): token xk sampled from qk is accepted with probability min(1, pk(xk)/qk(xk)). When accepted, the token provably follows the causal anchor distribution. On rejection, the token is resampled from the corrected distribution normalize(max(0, pk qk)) and all subsequent tokens are discarded; the next step becomes pure stride with no tokens to introspect. When all tokens are accepted, bonus token is sampled from the final anchor distribution, achieving stride N+1. This mechanism naturally adapts the effective stride per step without manual tuning. The formal procedure is given in Algorithm 1, with detailed step-by-step illustration in Appendix F. Lossless ISD with residual adaptation. ISD uses single model as both proposer and introspector, eliminating the need for separate draft model to train, maintain, or synchronize. 7 Preprint. While it can be used as standalone generative model, its single-forward drafting-andverification structure also makes it natural fit for self-speculative decoding. Inspired by the gated LoRA approach in multi-token prediction (Samragh et al., 2025), we gate the LoRA residual with per-token binary mask: [MASK] positions use base+LoRA weights to produce high-quality strided tokens, while introspection positions use base-modelonly weights. Critically, because the entire model uses strict causal attention, introspection positions cannot attend to token positionsthe causal anchor distribution is computed from base-only weights over base-only KV cache, identical to pure base AR forward pass. This guarantees that ISD introspects against the exact base AR distribution, making the output bit-for-bit lossless (Appendix G). Theoretical speedup analysis. Let pk denote the acceptance probability of the k-th strided token, and Pk = j=1 pj the cumulative acceptance probability. For ISD at stride N, the expected tokens per forward pass (TPF) is (see Appendix for derivation): TPFN = 2 + P1 + P2 + + PN2 2 PN1 . (3) At perfect acceptance (pk = 1), TPFN = recovers the theoretical maximum. At pk = 0, TPFN = 1 degenerates to AR. In the memory-bound decode regime, forward pass latency is approximately constant regardless of stride size, so wall-clock speedup TPF. With typical acceptance rates of 0.85, stride N=3 achieves TPF 2.32.4 with compute overhead of only 2meaning ISD translates most of the parallel decoding benefit into real throughput gain with minimal wasted compute. Our evaluations show that I-DLM achieves close-to-optimal speedup empirically (Section 4). 3. I-DLM Serving Stack: AR-Compatible Serving Prior DLMs rely on custom inference pipelines that forgo the optimizations accumulated in AR serving systems (2). Because I-DLM preserves strict causal attention, we integrate it directly into SGLang as drop-in extension, inheriting paged KV cache, continuous batching, and tensor parallelism without modification. We illustrate the key system optimizations below (ablated in 4.3). (1) AR-inherited optimizations. Each ISD step maps to SGLangs native extend mode, appending 2N1 tokens with causal attention. Unlike block diffusionwhere requests within batch must synchronize at the slowest converging block, breaking continuous batching (2)ISD produces at least one quality-guaranteed token every step, so all requests advance uniformly and continuous batching works unmodified. This is why I-DLMs throughput scales proportionally with concurrency while block diffusion methods plateau (Figure 2c). The causal structure further enables two key reuses. First, we capture the entire extend forward into single CUDA graph, replaying it each step with only input ids and attention metadata updated in-place. Second, since our extended sizes are small (9 tokens), we replace the three-kernel attention cascade with single paged-only kernel per layer, eliminating 2L redundant launches (Appendix D). ISD has strict dependency chain: forward verify (2) Stationary-batch scheduling. trim prepare forward. This prevents the CPUGPU overlap used in AR serving, making CPU overhead directly additive to step latency. We mitigate this with stationarybatch decode loop that reuses the batch object across consecutive ISD steps, bypassing the full scheduler rebuild. Within this loop, KV slots are allocated via single batched scatter, constant metadata is cached, and the ISD-specific KV trim-and-commit cycle frees rejected and MASK positions after each verification. Non-critical I/O is deferred to an overlap window during the next GPU forward. (3) Kernel fusion and proposal optimization. The verification step is fused into single Triton kernel with online softmax and Gumbel-max correction; the common accept path (78% of positions) returns after one streaming pass, skipping correction entirely. Since ISDs p/q criterion guarantees output correctness regardless of proposal quality, we use Preprint. argmax for proposals to maximize acceptance rate without affecting output diversity. For lossless R-ISD (Appendix G), we implement segment-gated LoRA where per-token binary mask gates the LoRA residual within the CUDA graph, with cuBLAS replacing segmented GEMV at small token counts and dedicated CUDA stream overlapping the LoRA shrink with base projections.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Evaluation Methodology We train two I-DLM variants: I-DLM-8B and I-DLM-32B, converted from Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B (Yang et al., 2025), respectively, using the all-masked causal training recipe (Section 3.1). Training uses 4.5B tokens on 8 H100 GPUs. For lossless ISD, we additionally train LoRA adapters (rank 128) on the same data. Full training details are in Appendix H. Baselines. We compare against two classes of state-of-the-art methods. (i) Diffusion LLMs: LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B)(Bie et al., 2026), LLaDA-2.0-flash (100B)(Bie et al., 2025), LLaDA-2.1flash (100B)(Bie et al., 2026), SDAR (8B, 30B-A3B)(Cheng et al., 2025), NBDiff (7B)(Tian et al., 2025), DREAM (7B)Ye et al. (2025), WeDLM (8B)Liu et al. (2025a), LightningRL (8B), TiDAR (8B), Jacobi Forcing (7B)(Hu et al., 2026), Fast-dLLM (7B)(Wu et al., 2025), Mercury Coder Small(Labs et al., 2025), and Gemini Diffusion; and (ii) Speculative decoding: EAGLE-3 (Li et al., 2025). We use our I-DLM AR counterparts, Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B, as baselines. Benchmarks. We evaluate on 15 benchmarks covering four domains: (i) Knowledge and Reasoning: ARC-C(Clark et al., 2018), MMLU(Hendrycks et al., 2020), MMLU-Pro(Wang et al., 2024), GPQA-D(Rein et al., 2024), GPQA(Rein et al., 2024); (ii) Math Reasoning: GSM8K(Cobbe et al., 2021), MATH-500(Hendrycks et al., 2021), MathBench(Liu et al., 2024), AIME-24(AIME), AIME-25(AIME); (iii) Code Generation: HumanEval(Chen et al., 2021), MBPP(Odena et al., 2021), LiveCodeBench-v6(Jain et al., 2024); and (iv) Instruction Following: IFEval(Zhou et al., 2023). All with thinking mode enabled. Metrics. For quality, we report accuracy on each benchmark with average values over three runs, where baseline results are taken from their original papers where available. For efficiency, we report request-level tokens per second (latency) and server-level tokens per second (throughput) under varying concurrency levels. Details in Appendix H. 4.2 End-to-End Performance Table 1 summarizes I-DLMs quality; ablations on different stride settings are in 4.3. I-DLM surpasses the quality of strongest DLMs with comparable sizes. I-DLM consistently outperforms all existing DLMs, often by large margins. At 8B scale, I-DLM-8B exceeds LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B) across all benchmarks despite using half the parameters, with particularly large gains on reasoning and code tasks (e.g., +26.3 on AIME-24 and +15.3 on LiveCodeBench-v6). Compared to SDAR, which is built on the same Qwen3-8B base, I-DLM improves dramatically (e.g., 69.6 vs. 10.0 on AIME-24). At larger scale, I-DLM-32B continues to outperform substantially larger models, surpassing LLaDA-2.1-flash (100B) by +16.7 on AIME-25 and +11.7 on LiveCodeBench-v6. Moreover, Table 2 shows that our I-DLM even outperforms proprietary DLMs on code generation, such as Mercury Coder Small (90.0 vs. 76.6) and Gemini Diffusion (89.6 vs. 76.0). I-DLM achieves comparable quality to AR models. Beyond outperforming prior DLMs, I-DLM is the first to match the quality of same-scale AR models. As shown in Table 1, I-DLM-8B achieves near-identical performance to Qwen3-8B, matching exactly on ARC-C, IFEval, remaining within 1 point on MMLU, even surpassing Qwen3-8b on Math-500. This result validates that introspective consistency is key missing ingredient in prior DLMs: by aligning generation with verification through logit-shifted causal training, I-DLM recovers AR-level capability. I-DLM delivers superior serving efficiency than existing DLMs. We evaluate end-to-end serving performance on MBPP, MATH-500, and LMSYS-Chat under concurrency levels 9 Preprint. Figure 5 shows the throughputlatency tradeoff. Across all workloads, I-DLM consistently outperforms prior DLMs at moderate to high concurrency. Starting from C4, I-DLM achieves higher per-request throughput than both SDAR (8B) and LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B), with the advantage widening as concurrency increases. At typical deployment scales (C=1632), I-DLM delivers 2.23.8 higher throughput than LLaDA-2.1-mini and 3.74.5 over SDAR. Under heavy load (C=64), I-DLM sustains stable per-request throughput (125 tok/s), translating to 2.94.1 higher throughput at C=64. Comparison with speculative decoding for AR models. Figure 5(b) compares I-DLM against EAGLE3 (Li et al., 2025), speculative decoding method that relies on an auxiliary draft model on top of the base AR model. I-DLM outperforms EAGLE3 in per-request throughput from C=1 through C=32 across all benchmarks (e.g., 341 vs. 238 tok/s on MATH-500, 319 vs. 221 on LMSYS-Chat, and 327 vs. 245 on MBPP at C=1). As concurrency increases, I-DLM maintains its advantage over baselines (199 vs. 176 tok/s on MATH-500 and 195 vs. 184 on LMSYS-Chat at C=32). 4.3 Ablation Studies Ablations of the training design. sections.title: A Detailed Related work
sections.content: Diffusion Language Models. Masked diffusion language models corrupt text by replacing tokens with [MASK] and train model to reverse the process (Austin et al., 2021; Sahoo et al., 2024; Lou et al., 2023). LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025a) scaled this paradigm to 8B parameters, LLaDA 2.0 (Bie et al., 2025) to 100B via mixture-of-experts, and LLaDA 2.1 (Bie et al., 2026) introduced token editing with confidence-based decoding. DREAM (Ye et al., 2025) introduced the logit shift technique, aligning the diffusion objective with the AR models logits[i] token[i+1] mapping. Block Diffusion (Arriola et al., 2025) generates fixed-size blocks autoregressively while denoising tokens within each block. Converting pretrained AR models into diffusion models is more data-efficient alternative. Gong et al. (2024) showed that fine-tuning with masked diffusion objective reduces training cost significantly. Self-Distillation Through Time (Deschenaux & Gulcehre, 2024) and Efficient-DLM (Fu et al., 2025) further streamline the pipeline. SDAR (Cheng et al., 2025) converts AR models via full-model training on 50B tokens for block-parallel generation, and NBDiff (Tian et al., 2025) extends this with causal prefix constraints. TiDAR (Liu et al., 2025b) proposes sequence-level hybrid that drafts via diffusion and verifies autoregressively. Unlike these approaches, I-DLM combines strict causal attention with logit-shifted prediction from MASK positionsby maximally respecting the pretrained AR models attention and prediction patterns, our conversion is far more data-efficient and closes the quality gap to the base AR model. Speculative decoding and multi-token prediction for LLMs. Speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023) accelerates AR models by drafting tokens with fast model and verifying them in parallel, provably preserving the target distribution. This guarantee relies on the target model having well-trained verify distribution pa property that AR models possess inherently but standard DLLMs lack due to mask-only training. Extensions include Medusa (Cai et al., 2024) (multiple prediction heads), the EAGLE family (Li et al., 2024; 2025) (feature-level drafting), and SpecInfer (Miao et al., 2023) (tree-structured verification). Multi-token prediction (MTP) trains models to predict multiple future tokens simultaneously (Gloeckle et al., 2024). Samragh et al. (2025) proposed gated sparse expert LoRA for MTPthe closest prior work to our conditional LoRA, though designed for multi-head prediction rather than diffusion verification. Consistency LLMs (Kou et al., 2024) achieve acceleration via Jacobi iteration. ISD differs from these by using single model with training-time stride capability to both achieve quality guarantee and deliver high latency and throughput gains under high concurrency. DLLM-specific decoding. FastDLLM (Wu et al., 2025) enables confidence-aware parallel decoding with KV cache reuse. Jacobi Forcing (Hu et al., 2025) distills diffusion models for fewer-step convergence but degrades at larger batch sizes. Free Draft-and-Verification (Wu & Zhang, 2025) explores self-speculative approaches. WeDLM (Liu et al., 2025a) reconciles diffusion with causal attention for KV cache reuse. These methods rely on confidencebased acceptance or iterative denoising, which either lacks formal quality guarantees or incurs high compute overhead; our ISD provides provable AR-quality output in single strideintrospection cycle. Despite these advances, fundamental gaps remain between DLLMs and AR models in training scalability, inference alignment, compute efficiency, and infrastructure compatibility. We analyze these gaps quantitatively in the next section. 15 Preprint.

sections.title: C Why Block Diffusion Requires a Separate KV Commit Pass
sections.content: Our SDAR inference uses SGLangs native DLLM support1. In this section we explain why block diffusion methods (SDAR, LLaDA) require mandatory KV commit forward pass after denoising, and why this overhead is difficult to eliminate. After denoising steps produce final tokens, separate forward pass with store kv=True must write these tokens into the KV cache so that subsequent blocks can attend to them. This pass produces zero new tokens and is mandatory even at p=1, capping TPF at N/2. natural optimization would fuse commit with the next blocks denoising in one forward: ]. However, this requires per-position mixed attention masks: committed [t1, . . . , tN (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) commit , M, . . . , (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) denoise tokens need bidirectional attention among themselves, while MASK tokens need blockcausal attention. Current attention kernels (FlashAttention, FlashInfer) support only single global causal flag and cannot mix bidirectional and causal attention within one forward pass. Implementing this would require custom attention kernels, doubled query size (2N), and complex batching logic for concurrent requests at different stages. SGLangs codebase confirms this: dllm is commit and dllm needs commit flags are defined but never activatedthe optimization was considered but abandoned. Because I-DLM uses strict causal attention throughout, no commit pass is needed. Each ISD step is standard extend operation that incrementally commits KV entries as in AR decodingno mixed masks, no custom kernels. 1https://github.com/sgl-project/sglang/pull/19044 17 Preprint.

sections.title: D Attention Kernel Overhead
sections.content: Block diffusion uses block-causal attention, which differs from the strict token-level causal mask that AR serving kernels are optimized for. In SGLang, the standard extend path uses cascade of three attention kernels per layer: (1) ragged attention among new tokens, (2) paged attention against the cached prefix, and (3) merge kernel to combine the two via log-sum-exp renormalization. This cascade is optimized for large prefills but wasteful for DLLM decode steps that process only N=45 tokens, where the ragged kernels advantage vanishes and the 3 kernel launch overhead dominates. Because I-DLM uses strict causal attention with small extended sizes, it can bypass the cascade and use single paged attention kernel per layer, reducing kernel launches from 3L to (where is the number of layers). Figure 7 shows the resulting forward latency comparison across concurrency levels. At low concurrency (C=1), the cascade overhead is modest (+4%), but it grows to +20% at C=64 as the additional kernel launch overhead compounds with batching. Figure 7: Attention kernel forward latency at varying concurrency. I-DLM (Paged, single kernel) vs. Block DLLM (Cascade, three kernels). The cascade overhead grows from +4% at C=1 to +20% at C=64.

sections.title: E Attention Mask Structure
sections.content: Figure 8 visualizes the attention mask used during I-DLM training, contrasted with SDARs block-causal mask. The input sequence is the concatenation [xt x0], where xt is the allmasked (noisy) region and x0 is the clean reference. The mask is composed of three components: Noisy self-attention (Mnoisy): Self-attention within the noisy region xt. In our setting (use regular causal=True), this is causal within each block: position in block attends only to positions in the same block. SDAR instead uses bidirectional attention within blocks. Cross-attention (Mcross): Cross-attention from noisy tokens to clean tokens. Each noisy block attends to clean tokens from all preceding blocks (b < b), providing conditional context from the clean reference. Clean self-attention (Mclean): Self-attention within the clean region x0. In our setting, this is strict token-level causal (q kv), preserving the AR attention pattern exactly. SDAR instead uses block-causal attention here. The final mask is = Mnoisy Mcross Mclean. sections.title: G Lossless ISD with Gated LoRA
sections.content: Figure 10 illustrates the gated LoRA mechanism used in Residual ISD (R-ISD). During each forward pass, token positions are partitioned into two types based on their input: [MASK] positions (proposals) activate the LoRA residual, while clean positions (introspection) use base-model-only weights. Because of strict causal attention, introspection positions cannot attend to any [MASK] positiontheir KV cache entries are computed entirely from base weights over clean tokens. Per-position computation. Table 6 summarizes the computation at each position type. The gated LoRA mechanism applies per-token binary mask m: each linear layer computes hj Wxj + 1[jM] BA xj, where 1[jM] is 1 for [MASK] positions and 0 otherwise. Training, Serving, and Evaluation Details H.1 Training Setup Base models and data. We train two I-DLM variants: I-DLM-8B and I-DLM-32B, converted from Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B (Yang et al., 2025), respectively, using the introspectiveconsistency training recipe described in Section 3.1. Our training codebase builds on the open-source SDAR framework(https://github.com/JetAstra/SDAR). The training data consists of 4.5B tokens of responses generated across reasoning datasets. All training is performed on 8 H100 GPUs. 20 Preprint. prefix introspect ˆx2 Wx ˆx3 Wx exact x1 Wx tk Wx propose Wx + ABx Wx + ABx q t1 Wx base-only weights = exact AR distribution base+LoRA weights aligned with Clean Introspect Exact Propose Base (W) LoRA (AB) Figure 10: Gated LoRA in Residual ISD (R-ISD). During single forward pass, [MASK] (propose) positions compute Wx + ABx using base+LoRA weights, producing proposal distributions q. Clean and introspect positions compute Wx using base-only weights, producing the causal anchor distribution pidentical to pure base AR forward pass. Because of causal attention, introspection positions never attend to [MASK] positions, so is computed entirely from base-only KV entries. This makes the output bit-for-bit lossless with respect to the base AR model. Training schedule. For I-DLM-8B, we train for 2 epochs with stride curriculum: the first epoch trains with stride N=2, and the second epoch trains with stride N=3. During the first two stride expansions (N=1 2 and N=2 3), we use fixed low scale of 0.2 for the cross-entropy loss on clean tokens to speed up masked token learning. Starting from the N=3 4 expansion, we switch to the auto-balanced loss scaling described in Eq. 2. For I-DLM-32B, we train for 1 epoch with stride N=2 with LoRA using rank 1024 and fixed scale of 0.2. We use N=4 for evaluation without additional full training as well. Hyperparameters. We use full fine-tuning with DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 2. The learning rate is 1 105 with cosine decay and warmup ratio of 0.03. We use per-device batch size of 1 with gradient accumulation steps of 4, yielding an effective batch size of 32 across 8 GPUs. The maximum sequence length is 4096 tokens. Training uses bf16 mixed precision. LoRA training. For lossless ISD (R-ISD), we additionally train LoRA adapters with rank 128 on the same data using the same hyperameters with learning rate of 2e-4. The inference follows the gated residual design described in Section 3.2. H.2 Hardware All experiments are conducted on NVIDIA H100 80GB SXM GPUs with NVLink interconnect. We use CUDA 12.9 with FlashInfer for attention computation. CUDA graphs are enabled for all configurations. Table 8 lists the ISD algorithm parameters used for each stride configuration. Speculative decoding baselines. Table 9 details the configurations for speculative decoding baselines. All baselines use Qwen3-8B as the target model and are served with SGLang. server parameters. Common parameters SGLang configurations: mem-fraction-static=0.85, attention-backend=flashinfer, disable-radix-cache=true (required for DLLM KV trim). Sampling parameters. For quality evaluation, all models use temperature t=1.0, topk=50, top-p=0.95 (matching the ISD algorithm configuration). For the AR baseline (Qwen38B/32B), the same sampling parameters are used to ensure fair comparison. For each benchmark, results are averaged over three runs. Answer extraction. For math benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME), we extract the final boxed{...} answer after stripping the <think>...</think> block. For multiple-choice benchmarks (ARC-C, MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GPQA), we extract the answer letter using the pattern ANSWER: [A-D]. For code benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP, LCB), we extract the last Python code block containing function definition and execute it against the provided test cases using sandboxed subprocess with 10-second timeout. For IFEval, we use the official Google evaluator after stripping thinking blocks. Baseline reproduction. Results for LLaDA-2.1-mini are reproduced using SGLang with the official configuration (block size=4, threshold=0.95, edit threshold=0.9). Results for SDAR are reproduced using SGLangs DLLM serving mode. Results for EAGLE-3 are reproduced using its SGLang integration. All other baseline results are taken from their original publications.
